source_summary: Existing Chart 01 / first-pass / Chart 02 caches were reused before new Ngram requests.
Curated public brand and institutional URLs were checked where network access allowed.
limitations: Frequency and web visibility are separate evidence types.
Search counts are not used as precise frequency.
Brand/platform examples are naming evidence, not corpus-frequency evidence.
Public institutional examples are illustrative, not exhaustive.
The final Ngram bucket ends at 2022.

naming_patterns.label: X + hub
naming_patterns.description: A domain, audience, resource, service, or institution is named as a centralized access point.

naming_patterns.label: hub + X
naming_patterns.description: Hub acts as a modifier meaning hub-like, central, systemic, or node-based.

naming_patterns.label: XHub / X-hub / HubX
naming_patterns.description: Hub is incorporated into a brand, platform, website, product, or service name.
naming_patterns.notes: Keep brand/platform examples separate from ordinary phrase-frequency terms.

naming_patterns.label: Institutional Access Point
naming_patterns.description: Hub names a campus, service, office, room, portal, or resource access point.

naming_patterns.label: Platform / Content Access
naming_patterns.description: Hub names a digital, informational, content, data, documentation, or community access point.

naming_patterns.label: Technical System
naming_patterns.description: Hub names a node, connector, integration point, or technical routing object.

brand_platform_inventory.name: GitHub
brand_platform_inventory.year_source: public reference pages report official public launch in April 2008
brand_platform_inventory.notes: Brand/platform example; do not treat as ordinary phrase frequency.

brand_platform_inventory.name: Pornhub
brand_platform_inventory.year_source: public reference pages report launch in May 2007
brand_platform_inventory.sensitivity_or_caution: Adult-content platform; use neutrally as optional naming-pattern evidence only.
brand_platform_inventory.notes: Useful only as a platform-era compound naming example.

brand_platform_inventory.name: Sci-Hub
brand_platform_inventory.year_source: public reference pages report founding in 2011
brand_platform_inventory.sensitivity_or_caution: Legally contested access platform; use only as naming-pattern evidence.
brand_platform_inventory.notes: Brand/platform-like form; not ordinary lexical frequency.

brand_platform_inventory.name: HubSpot
brand_platform_inventory.year_source: HubSpot story/about pages report founding in 2006
brand_platform_inventory.notes: Hub as brand prefix rather than suffix phrase.

brand_platform_inventory.name: HubPages
brand_platform_inventory.year_source: HubPages FAQ and launch press references report August 2006

brand_platform_inventory.name: Hugging Face Hub
brand_platform_inventory.year_source: Hugging Face Hub documentation; exact launch year not asserted here
brand_platform_inventory.sensitivity_or_caution: Approximate date; do not show as precise launch year without further verification.
brand_platform_inventory.page_title_observed: Hugging Face Hub documentation · Hugging Face

brand_platform_inventory.name: Hubstaff
brand_platform_inventory.year_source: Hubstaff about page reports origin in 2012
brand_platform_inventory.sensitivity_or_caution: Optional background example; not central to Chart 03.
brand_platform_inventory.page_title_observed: About Hubstaff | Time Tracking & Productivity Monitoring Software

hypothesis_evaluation.hypothesis: Hub has become a reusable naming component for platforms, rooms, services, resources, communities, and access points.
supporting_findings: X + hub has strong frequency support and strong search visibility support.
15 public institutional/campus/service examples were retained as naming evidence.
7 brand/platform examples were retained as platform-era naming forms.
Platform/content terms and technical terms remain separate from ordinary phrase-frequency interpretation.
findings_requiring_caution: Ngram underrepresents very recent web-native naming patterns.
Search visibility shows public presence, not exact frequency.
Brand names are recognition anchors and naming examples, not lexical-frequency evidence.
Some broad terms such as community hub and service hub are semantically mixed without local context.
counter_evidence_or_limitations: Hub + X appears less dominant and more technical/systemic than X + hub.
Some candidate terms are sparse or failed in Ngram despite visible modern web usage.
hypothesis_evaluation.recommended_chart03_focus: Focus on X + hub as the main naming format for access points, with brand/platform compounds as recognition anchors and hub + X as a smaller technical-system modifier pattern.
do_not_claim: Do not claim brand popularity equals lexical frequency.
Do not claim Pornhub defines the modern meaning of hub; it is only one neutral platform-era naming example.
Do not claim Ngram first nonzero year is a first attestation.
Do not claim all X + hub phrases are post-2000.
Do not claim every hub name has the same access-point meaning without context.

data_cautions: Do not read search visibility as exact frequency.
Do not treat brands as ordinary phrase-frequency terms.
Adult-brand examples should remain neutral and optional.
Modern naming patterns may be undercounted in printed-book corpora.
Ambiguous broad phrases need context before making semantic claims.
